brand_description: Loop Council is an original AI-assisted artist brand for jazzy alternative hip-hop, relaxed pocket rap, turntablism, warm boom-bap drums, bass-forward grooves, conversational MC interplay, and occasional guest-style verses with contrasting deliveries. The sound should feel clever, loose, musical, urban, communal, and deeply rhythmic without imitating any real artist, voice, cadence, flow, lyrics, scratches, beats, arrangements, persona, or copyrighted work.

audience.description: Listeners who like jazz rap, golden-era-inspired hip-hop, smooth MC flow, inventive sampling aesthetics, live-feeling basslines, DJ scratching, smart hooks, cypher energy, and records that feel both laid-back and technically sharp.
audience.guardrail: Suitable for general streaming audiences ages 16+. Allow mild profanity, street-level observations, social commentary, romance, ambition, humor, and competition. Avoid hate slurs, identity-based attacks, graphic violence, explicit sexual detail, self-harm instruction, drug-use instruction, or child-directed framing.

character.name: Loop Council
character.core_concept: A rotating hip-hop crew built around a crate-digging producer-DJ, a smooth lead MC, and occasional guest voices trading relaxed but precise verses over jazz-heavy beats.
character.fallback_summary: jazzy alternative hip-hop crew blending relaxed flow, boom-bap drums, turntable cuts, warm bass, and rotating MC energy
personality_traits: Communal and crew-oriented rather than lone-star posturing
Musical and sample-minded without copying source material
Cool under pressure, with subtle humor and social observation
Flexible enough for solo verses, cypher tracks, and guest-style contrasts
catchphrases: Pass the mic, move the room
character.visual_identity: Warm underground hip-hop visuals with turntables, record crates, street-corner jazz-club glow, subway-tile texture, handbill collage, speaker cones, and loose ink typography. Visuals should feel crate-digging, communal, urban, and musical without copying any real album cover or artist imagery.
visual_reference: Record crates, turntables, slipmats, mixer faders, speaker stacks, and subway-night texture
Warm tan, muted teal, deep brown, black vinyl, brass gold, and brick red palette
Jazz-club light, street flyers, hand-drawn arrows, and collage layers used tastefully
DJ booth and cypher-circle imagery used occasionally, not forced into every image
No celebrity likeness, no copied album layouts, no readable copyrighted logos

music.default_style: jazzy alternative hip-hop with smooth boom-bap drums, relaxed pocket flow, upright or electric bass, turntable scratching, and rotating MC textures
music.default_prompt: Original jazzy alternative hip-hop with warm boom-bap drums, loose swing, deep bass, Rhodes or jazz-guitar chords, horn or flute-like melodic accents, tasteful turntable scratching, and relaxed but precise rap vocals. Vary the lane by song: smooth lead-MC flow track, DJ-forward scratch showcase, crew cypher with mixed MC styles, mellow late-night jazz rap, upbeat block-party groove, socially observant head-nod track, and hook-forward radio-friendly single are all valid. Never imitate, clone, or reference any real artist voice, cadence, flow, lyrics, melody, beat, sample, scratch pattern, arrangement, persona, or copyrighted work. Create a fresh performance with pocket, wit, warmth, groove, clever MC interplay, and strong rhythmic identity.

lyrics.topic_variety: Use broad topic families: everyday city wisdom, creative confidence, friendship and crew loyalty, social observation without preaching, romance with humor, night travel, block-party joy, self-improvement, record-store or studio culture, and playful lyrical competition. Do not repeat the same crate, train, corner, jazz, pocket, vinyl, cypher, or microphone imagery across songs.
lyrics.required_closing: End with a memorable DJ cut image, final pocket line, conversational tag, crew handoff, warm fade, or unresolved street-scene detail; avoid preachy morals or generic goodbyes.

songwriting.primary_emotional_goal: Create original hip-hop songs that feel smooth, clever, musical, communal, and rhythmically addictive, with enough variation to support albums without repeating one beat, flow, or cypher setup.
songwriting.voice_perspective: Flexible first-person, direct address, rotating crew voices, or observational narrator. The lead voice should feel relaxed and precise; guest-style voices may contrast in tone, density, rhythm, or energy.

character_voice.person: Mostly first-person and direct second-person, with occasional collective 'we' for crew or neighborhood moments.
character_voice.tone: Cool, conversational, witty, rhythmic, observant, warm, and confident without being overly aggressive.
character_voice.vocabulary: Plainspoken urban detail, playful rhyme, jazz/music references used sparingly, clever turns, and clean rhythmic phrasing; avoid forced slang or fake dialect.
character_voice.comedy_mode: Dry wit, friendly flexing, clever reversals, and situational humor; punchlines should feel conversational rather than insult-heavy.
character_voice.kid_relationship: Not a children's brand; lyrics should be appropriate for teens and adults.

reference_artists_for_internal_vibe_only: A Tribe Called Quest-inspired jazz-rap warmth
songwriting.reference_safety: References are internal vibe anchors only. Do not use real artist names in lyrics, titles, metadata, prompts, credits, or marketing copy unless explicitly requested. Do not imitate vocals, cadence, flow, rhyme schemes, lyrics, melodies, sample choices, scratch phrases, beats, arrangements, personas, or copyrighted material. Use broad traits—warm jazz influence, relaxed pocket, smart flow, DJ craft, and crew contrast—as inspiration for an original Loop Council identity.
allowed_elements: relaxed lead-MC flow with precise pocket
guest-style verses with contrasting rhythm or tone
turntable scratching as hook, transition, or punctuation
warm boom-bap drums and loose swing
jazz chords, bass movement, horn stabs, flute-like accents, or Rhodes texture
social observation grounded in everyday detail
mellow hooks, chant refrains, or DJ-cut motifs
clean or radio-friendly language by default
forbidden_elements: direct imitation of any real artist cadence, flow, rhyme scheme, vocal tone, beat, sample, scratch phrase, arrangement, or persona
using real artist names in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata
sampling language that implies use of copyrighted recordings
fake dialect, caricatured slang, or cultural stereotyping
explicit sexual content or coercive framing
children's music framing, Pancake Robot references, or kid call-and-response
required_elements: Every song must have a distinct concept, emotional angle, groove lane, and hook design.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Catchphrases are optional spice, not mandatory lyrics. Use at most one in a song, and usually none.
Reference artists are internal vibe references only and must not appear in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata.
DJ scratches and turntable moments should vary by function: hook, transition, answer line, intro texture, or outro tag.
Use at least one concrete street, room, studio, movement, friendship, or object detail in each song.
render_safety: Lyrics must contain only words intended to be rapped, sung, or scratched as vocal-like phrases.
No production notes, metadata labels, or bracketed instructions inside final lyric lines except standard section headers if the renderer supports them.
Mild profanity is allowed, but hate slurs and identity-based demeaning language are not.
Do not write fake accent spellings; vocal identity should come through rhythm, pocket, and attitude.
structure_preferences: [DJ INTRO] -> [VERSE 1] -> [HOOK or CUT REFRAIN] -> [VERSE 2] -> [HOOK] -> [GUEST VERSE or SCRATCH BREAK] -> [FINAL HOOK] -> [OUTRO CUT]
[BASSLINE INTRO] -> [LEAD VERSE] -> [CALLBACK HOOK] -> [GUEST VERSE] -> [SCRATCH BRIDGE] -> [FINAL VERSE] -> [TAG]
song_family_types: Smooth lead-MC jazz-rap track with relaxed pocket, warm bassline, and understated hook.
DJ-forward scratch showcase where cuts answer the verses and shape transitions.
Crew cypher with two or three contrasting MC-style sections and a simple unifying refrain.
Mellow late-night head-nod track with softer keys, observational lyric, and conversational tone.
Upbeat block-party groove with chantable hook, brighter drums, and playful call-backs.
Socially observant boom-bap track with grounded detail, calm confidence, and no slogan-heavy preaching.
production_variation_matrix: Rotate drum feel: dusty boom-bap, crisp head-nod, loose swing, block-party bounce, stripped breakbeat, or live-band pocket.
Rotate bass role: walking bass, rubbery electric bass, upright-style movement, minimal sub pocket, melodic bass hook, or call-and-response with drums.
Rotate musical bed: Rhodes chords, jazz guitar, horn stabs, flute-like lead, vibraphone texture, organ pad, or sparse piano loop built as an original phrase.
Rotate vocal shape: smooth lead flow, conversational verse, denser guest-style verse, laid-back hook, chant refrain, DJ-cut response, or spoken intro tag.
Use scratches selectively; some songs should feature them heavily, others only as punctuation, intro, or outro.
tempo_energy_lanes: 78-86 BPM: mellow late-night head-nod, spacious bass, relaxed lyric density.
87-94 BPM: classic jazz-rap pocket, smooth flow, warm drums, balanced hook.
95-102 BPM: upbeat block-party bounce, brighter percussion, playful call-backs.
103-112 BPM: faster cypher energy, tighter verse tradeoffs, more DJ movement.
113-120 BPM: rare high-energy breakbeat or dance-adjacent lane, used for contrast.
arrangement_variation_rules: Rotate intro types across releases: scratch phrase, bassline, drum break, horn stab, spoken room tone, keyboard vamp, or cold rap line.
Every song should vary at least three major variables: tempo, drum feel, bass role, vocal delivery, hook type, guest presence, scratch function, or instrumental texture.
Do not always include guest-style MCs; use them when contrast improves the song.
Do not always make scratching the main hook; sometimes let bass, horn, keys, or vocal refrain carry it.
Avoid identical verse lengths, cypher order, hook density, scratch placement, and outro cut style across adjacent songs.
hook_variation_rules: Rotate hook types: mellow sung refrain, chant phrase, DJ-cut motif, bassline hook, call-back line, crew response, or instrumental break.
Do not reuse the same hook rhythm, title placement, scratch placement, or cadence shape across adjacent songs.
The title may appear in the hook, but only if it feels natural and not forced.
A hook should have one clear job: groove, wit, uplift, reflection, crew unity, dance-floor movement, or cool-down.
Use repetition for pocket and memorability, but change cuts, backing responses, bass movement, or adlibs on later returns.
lyrical_angle_variation_rules: Rotate point of view: lead MC reflection, crew cypher, street-corner observer, friend-to-friend advice, playful flirtation, creative-process story, or calm social critique.
Do not make every song about records, crates, jazz, or being an MC; those are lanes, not the whole brand.
When lyrics are socially observant, include human detail instead of abstract lectures.
When lyrics are playful or romantic, keep wit and warmth instead of generic flexing.
When lyrics are competitive, keep it clever and musical rather than cruel or violent.
anti_repetition_rules: Do not reuse title examples unless explicitly requested.
Do not repeat the same crate, train, corner, jazz, pocket, vinyl, cypher, mic, or sidewalk imagery across every song.
Do not force specific numbers, objects, symbols, crew lore, or DJ phrases; they are optional background texture only.
Avoid repeated opening moves such as crate digging, stepping to the mic, riding a train, or calling out the DJ.
Do not reuse the same drum break feel, scratch function, guest handoff, hook cadence, or emotional conclusion across multiple tracks.
Keep Loop Council identity mostly in metadata and visual world; do not name-drop the brand in every lyric.
album_sequence_guidance: Open with an accessible groove that establishes bass, pocket, and crew warmth without using the busiest cypher first.
Sequence solo lead-MC tracks beside guest-style cyphers and DJ-forward pieces so the album breathes.
Use scratch-heavy tracks as highlights or transitions, not every-track default.
Place the most socially observant track after a relaxed or playful song to avoid preachy pacing.
Save one high-energy crew track for the late-middle or final third.
End with either a warm instrumental fade, final DJ cut, conversational tag, or mellow reflective verse.
earworm_stack: Introduce a memorable bassline, drum pocket, scratch, horn phrase, or vocal cadence within the first 12 seconds.
Pair relaxed verses with a clean hook, DJ motif, or bassline identity.
Use pocket, swing, and small rhythmic surprises to create replay value.
Make at least one line per verse conversationally quotable without forcing punchlines.
Let guest-style contrast create variation without derailing the core groove.
hook_design_rules: Hooks should be smooth, rhythmic, DJ-cut-driven, bassline-sticky, or crew-friendly.
Avoid direct artist-reference language in hooks.
The title can appear in the hook but does not have to.
Use contrast between verse flow and hook simplicity.
For scratch-led lanes, let the DJ cuts function like a responding voice; for cypher lanes, let the refrain unify the MCs.

energy_arc.pattern: DROP -> POCKET -> HANDOFF -> CUT -> LIFT -> COOL-OUT
rules: Start with a recognizable groove, scratch, bassline, keyboard phrase, or vocal spark rather than a long empty intro.
Build energy through pocket and MC contrast before adding density.
Include at least one handoff or texture change: guest-style verse, DJ cut, beat dip, bass variation, or hook response.
Let the final section feel warmer, busier, calmer, or more communal depending on the song family.
End with a memorable cool-out rather than a generic fade.

participation_patterns: Crew response line for cypher tracks
Short chant phrase for block-party tracks
DJ-cut response used as a call-back voice
Bassline motif listeners can recognize before the chorus
Final conversational tag that reframes the groove
qa_rules: Do not include real artist names in finished lyrics, title, metadata, or audio prompt.
Reject any output that sounds like a direct A Tribe Called Quest or Q-Tip imitation rather than an original adjacent jazz-rap lane.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Every song must vary at least three of these: tempo, drum feel, bass role, scratch function, hook type, vocal delivery, MC count, or production texture.
If a song uses guest-style MCs, confirm each voice has a distinct rhythm, density, or emotional stance.
If a song leans heavily into jazz references, confirm it still has a clear hip-hop hook and modern listener entry point.
Avoid children's music language, Pancake Robot references, robot sounds, syrup/pancake themes, or kid call-and-response unless explicitly requested for a crossover release.

visuals.style: Warm underground jazz-rap cover art with record crates, turntables, mixer faders, street flyers, subway-night texture, speaker circles, handbill collage, and jazz-club glow. Polished enough for streaming artwork; not photorealistic.
visuals.negative_prompt: photorealistic, celebrity likeness, real artist portrait, copied album cover, explicit drug imagery, weapons, gore, hate symbols, children's cartoon branding, Pancake Robot character, readable copyrighted logos, dense text, messy typography, misspelled words, fake record label logos
visuals.text_overlay_style: loose underground hip-hop handbill typography, limited words, warm contrast, collage texture, no dense lyric blocks

distribution.content_advisory: clean or radio-friendly by default; mild profanity and social commentary allowed without hate slurs, graphic violence, or explicit sexual content
